did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) deep learning model for the multi-class classification of six skin disorders (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, and Nevus) from skin lesion images. It also compares the proposed CNN against pre-trained deep learning models such as DenseNet201, XceptionNet, and MobileNetV2.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for multi-class classification of skin disorders. It details a specific methodology including dataset acquisition (1373 patients, 2298 images), data augmentation, model architecture design, and training. It reports original results, including a classification accuracy of 98% on a test set, and compares these results against pre-trained models (DenseNet201, XceptionNet, MobileNetV2). Although published in conference proceedings (ICECAA 2022), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: An Automated and Efficient Deep Learning based Classification of Multiple Skin Disorders from Skin Lesion Images
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on Edge Computing and Applications (ICECAA 2022)
brief_summary: This study proposes an automated deep learning method for the multi-class classification of six skin disorders, including three malignancies (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma) and three benign conditions (Actinic Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, Nevus). The researchers utilized a publicly available dataset comprising 2,298 images from 1,373 patients acquired via smartphones. A custom shallow Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) was designed with data augmentation to handle class imbalance and high variability, achieving a classification accuracy of 98% on an unseen test set. The proposed model's performance was compared against pre-trained architectures such as DenseNet201, XceptionNet, and MobileNetV2. The results indicate that the custom CNN outperforms the pre-trained models in terms of accuracy while requiring significantly less training time (55 minutes versus 3-8 hours). The study concludes that the proposed efficient and accurate model is suitable for computer-aided diagnosis and early screening applications.

all_task_types.target: Six discrete skin disorder categories: Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Melanoma (MEL), Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), Actinic Keratosis (ACK), Seborrheic Keratosis (SEK), and Nevus (NEV).
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin lesions into six distinct diagnostic categories (three malignant cancers and three benign diseases) using a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). The model predicts discrete class labels, achieving 98% accuracy on an unseen test set. The task involves assigning each input image to one of the six predefined classes.

datasets_used.name: Publicly available skin lesion dataset (smartphone-acquired)
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset comprising patient details and clinical images acquired with smartphones. The dataset includes medical image data and medical evidence with up to 21 features. It is divided into six categories: three skin cancers (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma) and three skin diseases (Nevus, Actinic Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis).

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN model. The original dataset of 2298 images was augmented (random rotation, horizontal/vertical flipping, channel shifting, height/width shifting, zooming) to increase the training set size to 2592 images.
use.validation: Used for validation, containing 540 images from six different training sets.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance (reported accuracy of 98%). The specific count of test images is not explicitly stated, though it is part of the original dataset split.
use.other: The dataset was also used to compare the proposed model against pre-trained models (DenseNet201, XceptionNet, MobileNetV2).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the classification performance and identify real-positive values on an unseen test set of 540 images.
To monitor model convergence and performance during the 50 epochs of training, comparing training accuracy (99.92%) and validation accuracy (97.96%) to assess generalization.
To mitigate overfitting caused by class imbalance and limited sample sizes, and to regularize the model by increasing the training dataset size from 2298 to 2592 images.
To reduce overfitting and make the model less vulnerable to noise by randomly removing 20% of neurons during training.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices, with comparisons made based on absolute metric values rather than statistical significance.

Publicly available skin lesion dataset (.notes: The proposed custom CNN achieved the highest test accuracy (98%) and required the least training time (55 minutes) compared to pre-trained models. The composite index for the test set was 98%.

architecture_used.pretraining: None (Custom model trained from scratch; compared against pre-trained DenseNet201, XceptionNet, and MobileNetV2)

architecture_details.total_layers: Multiple layers including 6 convolutional layers, max-pooling layers, batch normalization layers, dropout layers, flatten layer, and dense layers
architecture_details.pooling_layers: Max pooling with 2x2 kernel size and stride 2

training_details.hardware: 24 GB graphics memory, 64 GB RAM, Windows OS

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation including random rotation, horizontal flipping, vertical flipping, channel shifting, height shifting, width shifting, and zooming

conclusions: The proposed custom shallow Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) achieved a classification accuracy of 98% on an unseen test set of 540 images, demonstrating high efficacy for multi-class classification of six skin disorders (three malignant and three benign).

The custom CNN model significantly outperformed pre-trained models (DenseNet201, XceptionNet, and MobileNetV2) in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency, requiring only 55 minutes of training time compared to 3-8 hours for the pre-trained models.

Data augmentation techniques (random rotation, flipping, shifting, and zooming) were effective in increasing the dataset size from 2298 to 2592 images, helping to mitigate overfitting caused by class imbalance and limited sample sizes.

The study highlights the scarcity of multi-class histopathological image datasets as a significant limitation for developing automatic diagnostic frameworks, noting that most existing works focus on binary classification or fewer classes.

The authors acknowledge that several limitations must be overcome before such systems can be fully employed in clinical practice, including the need to optimize hyper-parameters (optimizer, learning rate, filter size) and address issues such as blurred images due to inadequate lighting.
Early detection of skin cancer is critical for improving survival rates, and the proposed efficient deep learning method shows promise for computer-aided diagnosis and early screening applications.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, publicly available dataset of only 2,298 images from 1,373 patients, which limits the generalizability and robustness of the deep learning model.
The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance and high inter-class/intra-class variability, which poses challenges for generalization and increases the risk of overfitting.

Data augmentation was used to increase the training set size from 2,298 to 2,592 images, but the paper does not report specific augmentation parameters (e.g., rotation angles, zoom factors), limiting reproducibility.

The dataset consists of smartphone-acquired images rather than standardized dermoscopic images, introducing variability due to lighting conditions and image quality (e.g., blurred images due to inadequate lighting).

No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on a single unseen test set from the same source dataset, raising concerns about dataset bias and overfitting to specific image characteristics.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between the proposed model and pre-trained models (DenseNet201, XceptionNet, MobileNetV2), relying solely on absolute metric differences.
Hyperparameters such as optimizer, learning rate, and filter size were not optimized, which may have suboptimal performance compared to a fully tuned model.
The model was trained on a limited number of classes (six), whereas real-world clinical applications often require distinguishing between many more skin conditions.
The study acknowledges that several limitations must be overcome before clinical deployment, including the need for better preprocessing and larger datasets.
